ai_enrichment.summary: Dan é o quinto filho de Jacó e Bilha, e seu nome é associado à tribo de Dan, uma das doze tribos de Israel. A tribo de Dan teve um papel significativo na história de Israel, incluindo sua posição durante a jornada no deserto e suas conquistas na Terra Prometida. A cidade de Dan, também conhecida como Laís, foi capturada pelos danitas e se tornou um centro de idolatria. A história de Dan reflete temas de bênção, falhas e a complexidade da relação de Israel com Deus.
outline: Dan como o quinto filho de Jacó e Bilha
Atribuições e bênçãos da tribo de Dan
Conquistas e falhas da tribo de Dan
A cidade de Dan e sua importância histórica
Idolatria e comércio associados à tribo de Dan
key_concepts: Bênção e herança na tradição israelita
Identidade tribal e seu papel na história de Israel
Conflito entre fidelidade a Deus e práticas idólatras
ai_enrichment.theological_significance: A história de Dan ilustra a complexidade da aliança de Deus com Israel, mostrando tanto a bênção divina quanto as consequências da infidelidade. A tribo de Dan serve como um exemplo de como a desobediência pode levar à idolatria e à perda de identidade, refletindo a necessidade de fidelidade a Deus na vida do povo escolhido.

definitions.text: A judge. (1.) The fifth son of Jacob. His mother was Bilhah, Rachel’s maid (Gen. 30:6, “God hath judged me”, Heb. dananni). The blessing pronounced on him by his father was, “Dan shall judge his people” (49:16), probably in allusion to the judgeship of Samson, who was of the tribe of Dan. The tribe of Dan had their place in the march through the wilderness on the north side of the tabernacle (Num. 2:25, 31; 10:25). It was the last of the tribes to receive a portion in the Land of Promise. Its position and extent are described in Josh. 19:40-48. The territory of Dan extended from the west of that of Ephraim and Benjamin to the sea. It was a small territory, but was very fertile. It included in it, among others, the cities of Lydda, Ekron, and Joppa, which formed its northern boundary. But this district was too limited. “Squeezed into the narrow strip between the mountains and the sea, its energies were great beyond its numbers.” Being pressed by the Amorites and the Philistines, whom they were unable to conquer, they longed for a wider space. They accordingly sent out five spies from two of their towns, who went north to the sources of the Jordan, and brought back a favourable report regarding that region. “Arise,” they said, “be not slothful to go, and to possess the land,” for it is “a place where there is no want of any thing that is in the earth” (Judg. 18:10). On receiving this report, 600 Danites girded on their weapons of war, and taking with them their wives and their children, marched to the foot of Hermon, and fought against Leshem, and took it from the Sidonians, and dwelt therein, and changed the name of the conquered town to Dan (Josh. 19:47). This new city of Dan became to them a new home, and was wont to be spoken of as the northern limit of Palestine, the length of which came to be denoted by the expression “from Dan to Beersheba”, i.e., about 144 miles. “But like Lot under a similar temptation, they seem to have succumbed to the evil influences around them, and to have sunk down into a condition of semi-heathenism from which they never emerged. The mounds of ruins which mark the site of the city show that it covered a considerable extent of ground. But there remains no record of any noble deed wrought by the degenerate tribe. Their name disappears from the roll-book of the natural and the spiritual Israel.”, Manning’s Those Holy Fields. This old border city was originally called Laish. Its modern name is Tell el-Kady, “Hill of the Judge.” It stands about four miles below Caesarea Philippi, in the midst of a region of surpassing richness and beauty. (2.) This name occurs in Ezek 27:19, Authorize Version; but the words there, “Dan also,” should be simply, as in the Revised Version, “Vedan,” an Arabian city, from which various kinds of merchandise were brought to Tyre. Some suppose it to have been the city of Aden in Arabia. (See MAHANEH-DAN.)
